title: Soliton-antisoliton pairs in the supersymmetric gapped phase of an interacting Majorana chain
authors: Alberto Nocera, Mobin Shakeri, Armin Rahmani, Ian Affleck
abstract: A strongly interacting chain of Majorana fermions realizes the supersymmetric tricritical Ising phase, with supersymmetry (SUSY) extending into a symmetry-broken ordered phase adjacent to the tricritical point. Although the signatures of SUSY at the tricritical point are well understood, their behavior in the gapped phase remains less clear. Here, we address two key questions: how SUSY manifests in the gapped phase and what is the nature of the excitations in this phase. We show that, in the thermodynamic limit, a conventional SUSY diagnostic that remains finite at the tricritical point diverges immediately on the Ising side, yet decays continuously to zero deeper in the gapped phase, signaling the persistence of SUSY. Focusing on the lowest excited states in the supersymmetric gapped regime, we find that the excitations consist of soliton-antisoliton pairs separating distinct ordered regions. Each soliton binds an emergent localized Majorana mode, and together the pair forms a nonlocal Dirac fermion. The occupation of this Dirac mode distinguishes eigenstates with even and odd fermion parity.
summary: This work analyzes an interacting Majorana chain near a supersymmetric tricritical point, demonstrating that supersymmetry persists into the gapped phase. The key finding is that the low-energy excitations are soliton-antisoliton pairs, which bind localized Majorana modes and collectively form a nonlocal Dirac fermion. The study uses advanced numerical diagnostics to map out the phase boundaries and characterize these emergent quasiparticles.
main_problem: The paper investigates how supersymmetry (SUSY) manifests and what the nature of excitations are in the gapped phase adjacent to the supersymmetric tricritical Ising (TCI) point of an interacting Majorana chain.
main_result: SUSY is shown to persist in the gapped phase, evidenced by the continuous decay of a SUSY diagnostic ratio, and the low-energy excitations are identified as soliton-antisoliton pairs forming nonlocal Dirac fermions.
method: The study employs numerical techniques like DMRG, analyzing lattice supercharge operators and introducing symmetry-breaking perturbations to probe the system's ground and excited states.
model_or_system: The system is a strongly interacting chain of Majorana fermions, modeled by the O’Brien–Fendley (OF) Hamiltonian, which exhibits a transition between a critical Ising phase and a gapped, ordered phase.
key_observables: The SUSY diagnostic ratio ($R$), the energy splitting ($\Delta E_{0,1}$) of the first excited state, and the difference in Majorana Green's functions between even and odd fermion-parity sectors.
important_parameters: Interaction strength ($g/t$), the SA-pinning perturbation strength ($\Delta_{SA}$), and the separation distance ($m$) of the soliton-antisoliton pair.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the applicability of lattice supercharge operators flowing to field-theoretic operators and focuses on the lowest excited states.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase diagram, the behavior of the SUSY diagnostic ratio $R$ near the TCI point, and the energy dependence of the first excited state on the SA-pinning field.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the physical model and the SUSY diagnostic ($R$), then characterizing the TCI transition, and finally using SA-pinning perturbations to confirm the existence and nature of the soliton-antisoliton excitations in the gapped phase.
why_interesting: The connection between topological order, emergent supersymmetry, and localized excitations (solitons) in a 1D strongly correlated system provides a rich theoretical playground relevant to understanding quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: (a) The numerically computed value of R defined in Eq. (6) as a function of g/t for various system sizes. A kink appears on the Ising side, which extrapolates toward the TCI point. (b) Finite-size scaling of the position of the kink.

Figure 4: The energy densities of the ground and first-excited states in the two fermion parity sectors for g/t in the ordered phase [panel (a)] and in the Ising phase [panel (b)]. The ground-state energy exhibits a linear dependence on ∆in the or- dered phase for small ∆. A positive (negative) ∆lowers the energy in the even (odd) fermion parity sector, leading to a unique ground state with even (odd) parity. These results were obtained with the energy scale t set to one.

Figure 5: Density of Dirac fermions for positive ∆(panel a) and negative ∆(panel b) in each parity sector. The value of |∆| has been taken smaller than the level crossing shown in Fig. 4(a). Regardless of the sign of ∆, the even and odd parity sectors have 〈nc〉≃0, 〈nd〉≃1/2 and 〈nc〉≃1/2, 〈nd〉≃0, respectively.

Figure 6: Panel (a): Ground and first excited state energies as a function of ∆SA (with SA distance m = L/2). Inset: wave-function overlaps as a function of ∆SA, showing clear evidence of a level crossing. Panels (b–c): Density of Dirac fermions for values of ∆SA below and above the level crossing observed in panel (a).

Figure 7: (a–b) Ground-state and first excited state energies in the two fermion parity sectors as a function of the soliton location m. All data are for L = 120 Dirac fermions and SA pinning field ∆SA = 0.001. The top, middle, and bottom panels correspond respectively to the Ising phase (g/t = −0.410), the TCI point (g/t = −0.428), and the gapped phase (g/t = −0.446). (c) The difference ∆E0,1 = |E0,1 even−E0,1 odd| between ground states (first excited states) of the two parity sectors at fixed SA locations m close to the maximum m = L/2 as a function of g/t.
